identity.title: Coastal and Great Lakes Freight Transportation

lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market operators providing recurring or repeat outsourced cargo transportation in coastal waters, on the Great Lakes System, or between US domestic ports, including crewed coastal and Great Lakes barge and vessel charter services.
lens.excluded_activities: Deep-sea foreign freight, inland freight outside the Great Lakes System, harbor tug services, passenger operations, captive shipper fleets, dormant vessel-owning shells, non-control financing vehicles, and firms without transferable operating organizations.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Business and government shippers buy voyages, contracts of affreightment, or time charters, with a mix of annual or multi-year term contracts and market-priced spot movements; the carrier supplies vessels, qualified crews, compliance, and accountable transport execution.

a.rationale: The water-transportation occupation mix places 18.84% of employment in office and administrative support, including customer service, freight-agent, dispatch, bookkeeping, and general-clerk roles, and another 5.72% in business and financial operations. Document intake, quotation support, billing, customer communications, scheduling assistance, compliance drafting, and exception triage expose meaningful task shares, while licensed navigation, engineering, deck work, cargo handling, maintenance, and emergency response remain physical and safety-critical. The range is a wage-weighted task judgment rather than an occupation-level displacement assumption.
a.change_evidence: A firm-level time study mapping payroll by occupation to voyage quoting, dispatch, documentation, billing, compliance, maintenance planning, and onboard watchstanding, paired with system logs showing which tasks are already automated, would materially change this range.
caveats: The BLS occupation mix covers NAICS 483000 rather than 483113 and includes passenger and inland operators.
Employment shares are not wage shares or task shares, and no source directly measures current generative-AI exposure in coastal freight firms.
The estimate excludes already-automated work and does not assume autonomous vessels replace licensed crews within five years.

rho.rationale: More than half of the exposed shore-side opportunity could plausibly be implemented through copilots, workflow integration, human approval, and improved data capture within five years. Implementation is constrained by fragmented vessel and accounting systems, customer-specific documents, cybersecurity, auditability, and the need for qualified people to retain control of safety-critical decisions. GAO finds current commercial autonomous-vessel uses narrow and generally subject to human control, supporting a conservative implementation assumption for onboard work.
rho.change_evidence: Twelve months of before-and-after workflow data from several LMM coastal carriers, including error rates, manual overrides, customer acceptance, Coast Guard findings, cyber-control testing, and realized avoided hires by task, would materially change this estimate.
caveats: Implementation evidence is not specific to generative-AI deployments at LMM coastal freight operators.
Cybersecurity and safety-management controls may slow adoption even for shore-side workflows.
The estimate applies only to the opportunity counted in a, not to total industry labor.

e.rationale: The official industry definition is already centered on outsourced cargo transport and crewed chartering, so a majority of economically active LMM firms should fit the service lens. Eligibility is reduced for captive fleets, vessel-holding shells, firms dependent on one nonrepeat project, distressed or noncompliant fleets, and businesses whose permits, customer approvals, crewing, or Jones Act ownership cannot transfer cleanly. Kirby's description of integrated companies, small operators, and captive fleets confirms that ownership models are mixed.
e.change_evidence: A named-firm census of the 85 estimated LMM firms, with revenue source, customer recurrence, fleet ownership, US citizenship and coastwise endorsements, safety record, customer concentration, captive status, and transferable management depth, would materially change eligibility.
caveats: The provided firm count is an estimate derived from receipts and margins, not an observed roster.
NAICS is assigned at establishment level while eligibility is a firm-level control-transfer concept.
Jones Act citizenship and vessel requirements can restrict buyer structures and post-close operating flexibility.

s5.bridge: The sources cover US employer-business owners and a voluntary cross-industry survey of 1,162 US owners, not LMM coastal freight owners. Census reports 51% of responding employer-business owners were at least 55, while the Exit Planning Institute reports 49% of respondents wanted to exit within five years. I translate exit intent and owner age into a much lower qualifying-transfer probability by discounting for internal succession without a control sale, delayed plans, closures, nontransferable operations, failed deals, and survey selection; the proxy likely overstates near-term completed transfers because exit-planning respondents are unusually engaged.
s5.rationale: Capital intensity, regulated operations, customer vetting, and specialized crews can support transferability but lengthen diligence and financing. Broad owner demographics imply succession pressure, while only a fraction of intended exits will become completed qualifying control transfers in the five-year window.
s5.change_evidence: An industry-specific owner-age and succession survey combined with five years of closed private-company control transactions from Coast Guard vessel-documentation changes, Capital IQ, PitchBook, GF Data, and maritime brokers would materially change the estimate.
caveats: Neither source isolates transportation, maritime firms, the LMM EBITDA band, or completed control transfers.
The Census age statistic uses 2018 data and covers responding owners rather than all owners.
Exit intent is not a transaction, and an exit can be a closure or internal succession that does not qualify.

q.bridge: Kirby's coastal business is a large public-company operator rather than an LMM sample, and its reported term/spot mix measures contract structure rather than retained AI benefit. I map the reported predominance of term contracts and time charters to slower initial repricing, then discount retention over five years for mostly annual contract terms, renewal negotiation, explicit cost escalators, shipper procurement, and spot-market competition. Large-operator contracting may overstate term coverage and bargaining strength relative to small firms, biasing retention upward.
q.rationale: Term charters can preserve early labor savings because customers buy vessel availability and accountable transport rather than itemized administrative hours. Yet most term contracts by revenue are one year, renewals expose margins to repricing, and contractual escalators demonstrate that customers already negotiate how operating-cost changes are shared. Retention should therefore decay meaningfully across the five-year period rather than remain near full benefit.
q.change_evidence: Redacted LMM customer contracts and renewal histories showing rate formulas, open-book provisions, productivity commitments, bid frequency, fuel and labor escalators, plus gross-margin retention following documented process savings, would materially change this range.
caveats: One public operator may not represent LMM coastal, Great Lakes, dry-bulk, or general-cargo contracting.
The filing reports fuel and other price mechanisms, not AI-related savings pass-through.
Customer concentration and competitive tendering can make retention vary widely by route and vessel class.

d5.bridge: BLS projects employment of water-transportation workers, not constant-price, constant-quality demand for NAICS 483113, to rise 1% from 2024 to 2034, while BTS projects all-mode US freight tonnage, not coastal water freight, to rise 50% from 2020 to 2050. I use the near-flat water-employment outlook as the central five-year quantity signal and the broader freight forecast only as an upside boundary; productivity can make employment understate demand, while mode shifts and declining petroleum or coal movements can make all-mode tonnage overstate coastal demand.
d5.rationale: Current coastal utilization and customer demand are strong in Kirby's tank-barge segment, but the service basket spans volatile petrochemicals, refined products, coal, fertilizer, grain, and other bulk cargo. Pipeline substitution and decarbonization pressure some movements, while overall freight growth, limited vessel supply, and essential coastal distribution support roughly stable real service quantity over five years.
d5.change_evidence: FAF mode-specific 2026-to-2031 water tonnage by commodity and domestic route, USACE Waterborne Commerce series, customer production forecasts, and contracted vessel-days for a representative coastal and Great Lakes operator panel would materially change this range.
caveats: Employment is an imperfect quantity proxy because productivity, utilization, and crew rules can change independently of freight demand.
The BTS forecast is all-mode US freight and its horizon is much longer than five years.
Commodity and route mix can overwhelm aggregate trends, especially for coal and petroleum movements.

o.bridge: GAO and Coast Guard sources establish current US-wide legal, safety, and crewing constraints rather than measuring the year-five quantity share for NAICS 483113. I translate narrow commercial autonomy, statutory minimum crews, required credentialed masters and crew complements, and owner/operator safety responsibility into a high operator-required share. The evidence may overstate durability if law changes quickly or remote operation gains approvals, but the five-year horizon and capital replacement cycle limit that error.
o.rationale: Cargo may shift among vessels, pipelines, rail, and trucks, but quantity that remains coastal or Great Lakes water freight still requires an accountable carrier to own or control compliant vessels, provide qualified crews, manage safety and watchkeeping, and answer to customers and regulators. AI and autonomy can reduce or augment tasks, yet current commercial autonomous uses are narrow and US statutes still constrain crew reductions.
o.change_evidence: Coast Guard approvals for reduced-crewing autonomous commercial cargo vessels, statutory amendments, insurer and classification-society acceptance, and signed shipper contracts replacing conventional operators with remote or self-service models would materially change the estimate.
caveats: The proxy addresses legal and operational accountability more directly than customer self-service or modal substitution.
Some operator functions could migrate to remote operations centers without eliminating an accountable operator.
The estimate conditions on year-five service quantity; demand loss and mode substitution belong in d5.

narrative.executive_view: Coastal and Great Lakes freight combines a modest shore-side AI opportunity with unusually durable need for regulated, accountable vessel operators. The opportunity is concentrated in customer service, voyage administration, dispatch support, billing, documentation, compliance preparation, and maintenance planning rather than crew elimination. Contracting can preserve some early savings, but annual renewals and sophisticated shippers should reclaim a meaningful share over five years.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can structure shipping instructions, draft quotations and voyage documents, reconcile bills, answer routine status questions, summarize weather and maintenance records, flag compliance exceptions, and assist schedulers. Human approval remains important, and licensed masters, engineers, deck crews, and managing operators retain responsibility for navigation, cargo, emergency response, and safe manning. Autonomous-vessel adoption is unlikely to remove that operating core within five years.
narrative.value_capture: Time charters and term contracts sell vessel availability and service outcomes, so administrative productivity need not be passed through immediately. However, many contracts renew annually, spot moves reprice at market, and established fuel and labor escalators show customers already negotiate cost sharing. Retention depends on translating savings into reliability, capacity, and avoided hires before procurement cycles reset rates.
narrative.firm_availability: The industry definition fits outsourced recurring transport, but the 85-firm LMM count is modeled and not a verified target list. Captive fleets, holding shells, weakly compliant operators, single-customer assets, and firms with citizenship or endorsement transfer issues reduce eligibility. Broad US owner-age and exit-intent data suggest succession supply, but closed maritime control-transfer evidence is missing.
narrative.demand_durability: Five-year real quantity is likely roughly stable, with upside from general freight growth and constrained vessel supply but downside from commodity cycles, pipeline substitution, decarbonization, and modal shifts. Demand varies sharply across petrochemicals, refined products, coal, fertilizer, grain, and Great Lakes bulk trades. For freight that remains on the water, regulatory crewing and safety accountability make full software substitution unlikely.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The largest uncertainties are the absence of a 483113-specific task study, an unverified LMM target roster, cross-industry succession proxies, and reliance on one large operator for contract evidence. Safety incidents, cyber failures, customer concentration, vessel age, dry-dock needs, environmental rules, commodity exposure, and Jones Act constraints can dominate administrative savings. A weak route or an undercapitalized fleet can make the opportunity unattractive even when AI workflows perform well.
narrative.principal_driver: Regulated operator accountability and term-charter revenue make shore-side productivity improvements implementable without threatening the core service.
narrative.principal_weakness: The addressable AI task share is modest and the evidence for eligible, transferable LMM firms is indirect.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS Definition: 483113 Coastal and Great Lakes Freight Transportation
sources.supports: Defines the industry as cargo transportation in coastal waters, the Great Lakes System, or deep seas between US domestic ports, and includes coastal and Great Lakes barge transport and crewed chartering.

sources.title: Water Transportation - May 2023 OEWS Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates
sources.supports: Reports the NAICS 483 occupation mix, including 18.84% office and administrative support employment, 5.72% business and financial operations, and detailed customer-service, freight-agent, dispatcher, bookkeeping, and clerk employment.

sources.title: Coast Guard: Autonomous Ships and Efforts to Regulate Them
sources.supports: Finds current commercial autonomous-ship uses are narrow and generally retain human control; describes safety, cyber, legal, and statutory minimum-crewing constraints in US waters.

sources.title: Kirby Corporation 2025 Annual Report
sources.supports: Reports coastal contract mix, time charters, renewal terms, fuel escalators, utilization, commodity demand drivers, industry ownership mix, regulation, competition, and coastal operating conditions.

sources.title: 2023 National State of Owner Readiness Report
sources.supports: Reports that 49% of 1,162 surveyed US business owners wanted to exit within five years, a cross-industry intent proxy rather than completed-transfer evidence.

sources.title: Business Owners' Ages
sources.supports: Reports that 51% of responding owners of employer businesses were age 55 or older in the 2019 Annual Business Survey using 2018 data.

sources.title: Water Transportation Workers: Occupational Outlook Handbook
sources.supports: Projects water-transportation-worker employment to grow 1% from 2024 to 2034 and identifies bulk-commodity demand as a key employment driver.

sources.title: Freight Activity in the U.S. Expected to Grow Fifty Percent by 2050
sources.supports: Reports FAF5 projections for 50% growth in total US freight tonnage from 2020 to 2050 and notes forecasts vary by mode and economic scenario.

sources.title: US Coast Guard: Credentials, Documents and Records
sources.supports: States that owners, managing operators, and masters are responsible for safe manning and watchkeeping, and that vessels must carry the credentialed crew complement required by the certificate of inspection.

sources.title: MARAD Domestic Shipping: The Jones Act
sources.supports: States that domestic waterborne merchandise transportation requires a US-built, US-owned, Coast Guard coastwise-endorsed vessel, subject to limited statutory waivers.
